title: Shift is Good: Mismatched Data Mixing Improves Test Performance

reviews.summary: The paper studies when training on a mixture of subpopulations with proportions different from those used at test time can improve test performance, even when unlimited data from the test mixture is available. It formalizes this “positive distribution shift” for several learning-curve models (orthogonal power laws, coupon-collector-like memorization, and transfer models), derives optimal training mixtures and quantifies gains in test loss and sample complexity, and shows a general “almost always better to mismatch” result under a conservation condition. A small synthetic language-model experiment on skill composition illustrates that mixing a uniform-skill dataset with the test-task dataset can substantially reduce token requirements to reach a target accuracy.
strengths: Provides a unified, principled treatment of data-mixture mismatch, identifying optimal training mixtures q* for multiple regimes: orthogonal power-law learning curves, orthogonal memorization (coupon-collector-like), and transfer-learning-inspired models.

The “almost always better to mismatch” theorem (via a conservation condition) is conceptually strong, clarifying when matching train and test mixture is suboptimal except in measure-zero or highly constrained cases.
The decomposition argument for the standard transfer model that isolates q-independent transfer terms is elegant and clarifies when mixture optimization reduces to optimizing orthogonal components.
The synthetic skill-composition experiment connects theory to an LLM training scenario and shows large speed-ups when using a mismatched mixture, supporting the paper’s central message.
The problem setup and high-level intuitions are clearly motivated with an accessible example.
The paper distinguishes several learning-curve regimes and states propositions/theorems quantifying improvements and identifying q*.
The results are directly relevant to data curation and mixture optimization for modern large-scale training (LLMs, VLMs, multi-domain setups).
The work formalizes a practically observed phenomenon within a clean framework, potentially shaping future data-mixing practices and tooling.
weaknesses: Some expressions appear to have typos or unusual dependencies (e.g., the q* in Proposition 3.2 depends on N; feasibility and simplex constraints in Proposition 6.3 are under-specified; a likely typo in L_N(p, q) definition uses S ~ D_p^N instead of D_q^N).

The “almost always better to mismatch” section assumes differentiability and structural properties of subpopulation loss maps; the plausibility and scope of the conservation condition could be discussed more carefully, and the K=2 case is not treated with the same generality.

Empirical validation is limited to a single synthetic LLM setting; no real-world multi-domain or multi-task benchmarks, and no comparison to alternative mixture-optimization strategies or ablations (e.g., different α, K, or p).
The mixture policy used in the experiment (30% uniform + 70% test) is not justified as optimal under the presented theory nor compared against learned q* or alternative heuristics.
Information about variance, multiple runs, sensitivity to seeds, and hyperparameter choices is not presented in the main text.
A few equations or ranges likely contain typographical errors (e.g., the error function in the introductory figure caption, bounds for δ_N in Theorem 4.2, and the training distribution in L_N).
Practical constraints for q* (non-negativity, simplex feasibility, and projection when closed-form solutions fall outside the simplex) are not fully addressed.

While several relevant works are cited, a broader connection to domain adaptation/importance weighting literature (e.g., covariate shift correction via reweighting) and curriculum/domain-mixing optimization literature could further situate the contribution.
Empirical comparisons to recent data-mixture optimization methods (e.g., adaptive mixture tuning or importance weighting baselines) are absent.
reviews.questions:
1. In Proposition 3.2, q* depends on N when α_i differ. How should practitioners set mixtures over the course of training? Do you recommend schedule-based q(t) as N grows, and can you quantify the benefit of such schedules versus a fixed q?
2. In the main text, L_N(p, q) is defined with S ~ D_p^N (lines 68–70), which seems like a typo since training uses D_q. Please confirm and correct; does this affect any derivations?
3. For Model 6.2 (Proposition 6.3), how do you enforce q ∈ Δ_K in the presence of the closed-form solution? When q* falls outside the simplex, do you project, and if so, how does the theory change?
4. The “almost always better to mismatch” result assumes smoothness/differentiability for extending ē_k off the simplex. Can you clarify the exact regularity assumptions and whether K=2 admits an analogous result?
5. In the skill composition experiment, how was the 30%/70% mixture chosen? Did you sweep the mixture coefficient or attempt to optimize it? Could you report sensitivity, multiple seeds, error bars, and compare to a data-driven learned mixture baseline?
6. In Theorem 4.2 (and related expressions), there appear to be typographical issues (e.g., the interval for δ_N and possibly the definition of K_N). Could you provide a concise proof sketch and correct statements in the main text to aid verification?
7. Can you connect the optimal mixtures in the memorization model to practical guidance for large-scale pretraining (e.g., skill or domain catalogs with heavy-tailed frequencies)? How would you estimate or track p and learning curves online?
reviews.assessment: This paper offers a clear and useful theoretical lens on when and why training on mismatched data mixtures can outperform matched mixtures, with explicit optimal-mixture characterizations under several learning-curve models and a compelling “almost always better to mismatch” argument. The work is timely and practically relevant to data curation and scaling-law-guided training. The principal limitations are the modest empirical validation (one synthetic LLM study without sweeps, error bars, or real-world datasets) and several presentation/technical details that need tightening (typos in definitions/equations; more explicit feasibility handling in the exponential transfer model; clearer assumptions in the measure-zero theorem; discussion of K=2). Despite these gaps, the theoretical insights are valuable and novel, and the paper’s message is important for the community. With improved empirical validation and polishing of technical statements, this would be a strong contribution. I recommend a weak accept, encouraging the authors to strengthen experiments, correct minor errors, and clarify assumptions.
